Table 6: Point estimates (β̂) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) for five estimation methods and the male population across different age groups.

Figure 1: Comparison of Bias and SMSE for five estimation methods with a covariate dimension of p = 4 across sample sizes n ∈ {500, 1000, 2000, 4000}. The results are averaged over 500 replications. In each subfigure, the bars from left to right represent the metrics for the five methods: our proposed robust methods (R-NP and R-LR), the moment method (M), and the regression imputation methods (Reg-NP and Reg-LR).

Figure 2: Comparison of Bias and SMSE for five estimation methods with a covariate dimension of p = 10 across sample sizes n ∈ {500, 1000, 2000, 4000}. The results are averaged over 500 replications. In each subfigure, the bars from left to right represent the metrics for the five methods: our proposed robust methods (R-NP and R-LR), the moment method (M), and the regression imputation methods (Reg-NP and Reg-LR).
Table 1: Comparison of Neyman-orthogonal score construction frameworks: Proposed, Riesz Representer ([3]), and Concentrating-out ([1]).
Table 4: Point estimates (β̂) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) for five estimation methods and the pooled population across different age groups.

Figure 3: Proposed R-NP estimates and 95% confidence intervals for the causal effects of Medicaid coverage on four outcomes in the OHIE dataset. Subfigure (a) presents results for the pooled population, while (b) and (c) focus on females and males, respectively. In each panel, colors represent subgroup estimates by age: indigo for individuals aged ≤35, blue for ages 36–49, green for ages ¿49, and pink for the total group within that population.
